revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神人]]
{{Otheruses|Shinto priest|{{ill|Arahitogami|en|Arahitogami|qid=Q521834|lt=Hitogami (人神)|1=Arahitogami}}}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2, 2014|sortkey=Okinawa shinin}} '''Shinin''' (神人) is a term for a [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priest]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, including the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|ja|琉球諸島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Islands|1=Ryukyu Islands|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10905515}}. The meaning of the term varies based on pronunciation.
; Jinin, Jinnin : A lower-Ranking shrine priest or servant attached to a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}, performing miscellaneous duties. ; Shinjin, Kamibito : A term for {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannushi|qq=}}, mainly in classical texts. ; Kaminchu : A priest in {{ill|Ryukyuan religion|en|Ryukyuan religion|qid=Q892785|lt=Ryukyu Shinto|1=Ryukyuan religion}}, in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}} and the {{ill|Amami Islands|en|Amami Islands|qid=Q453510|lt=Amami Islands|1=Amami Islands}} of {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|qid=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}}.
== Lower-Ranking Shrine Priests and Servants == '''Shinin''' (じにん, じんにん) refers to lower-Ranking [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priests]] or {{ill|Yoribito|ja|寄人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoribito|1=yoryūdo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1453179}} who assisted {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} administrations in ritual services and maintenance duties from ancient to medieval times. They were also known as '''Shanin''' (社人).
Shinin were categorized into '''Honzha Shinin''', who were directly affiliated with shrines, and '''Sanzai Shinin''', who were spread across various shrine estates.
Shinin carried weapons for shrine and festival security and, from the late Heian period through the Muromachi period, were often involved in disorderly conduct alongside {{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|qid=Q1475695|lt=Sohei|1=Sōhei}}, including acts of violence and {{ill|Goso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goso|qq=}}. Beyond being armed groups, shinin included shrine-associated artisans, performers, merchants, and farmers, many of whom organized themselves into guilds known as {{ill|Za (guilds)|en|Za (guilds)|qid=Q2575257|lt=Za (guilds)|1=Za (guilds)}}. By doing so, they resisted control from {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|qid=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi (official)}}, {{ill|Shoen lords|ja|荘園領主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoen lords|1=shōen ryōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q129290035}}, and {{ill|Local samurai rulers|ja|在地領主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Local samurai rulers|1=zaichi ryōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11423927}}.
The '''Horikawa Shinin''' of {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|qid=Q692714|lt=Gion Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}} (formerly Gion-sha), based in {{ill|Gojo-Horikawa, Kyoto|ja|五条堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojo-Horikawa, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, engaged in the timber trade, floating logs down the {{ill|Hozu River|ja|保津川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozu River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 保津峡|1=Hozu Gorge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10887680}} from the {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|qid=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} mountains and stockpiling them in Gojo-Horikawa. Gion Shrine also had lower-status '''Inujinin''' (犬神人), who were tasked with shrine cleaning, security for festival floats, city-wide sanitation, and burial services.
The '''Hiyoshi Shinin''' of {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|qid=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}} leveraged their connection with {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|qid=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}} to engage in rice tribute transport and high-interest money lending to Kyoto aristocrats and provincial officials.
The '''Iwashimizu Shinin''' of {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|qid=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|1=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}} held monopolistic rights over the fish market in {{ill|Yodo-juku|en|Yodo-juku|qid=Q3572390|lt=Yodo, Kyoto|1=Yodo-juku}}, as well as water and land transport. The affiliated '''Oyamasaki Shinin''' of {{ill|Rikyu Hachiman Shrine|ja|離宮八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rikyu Hachiman Shrine|1=Rikyū Hachimangū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11659484}} controlled the exclusive purchase of {{ill|Perilla oil|ja|荏胡麻油|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Perilla oil|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to エゴマ油|1=Perilla oil|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q921630}}, forming the {{ill|Oyamasaki Oil Guild|ja|大山崎油座|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyamasaki Oil Guild|1=Ōyamazaki Aburaza|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11434614}}.
The '''Gusainin''' (供祭人) of {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂別雷神社}} and {{ill|Shimogamo Shrine|ja|下賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimogamo Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 賀茂御祖神社}} worked in the shrines' {{ill|Mikuriya|ja|御厨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuriya|1=mikuriya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488831}} estates. Those in the neighboring provinces of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|qid=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}} and {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|qid=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}} engaged in fishing and contributed seafood offerings, holding exclusive fishing rights in areas like the shores of {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|qid=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa|1=Lake Biwa}}.
At {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, {{ill|Jingu Eji|ja|神宮衛士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Eji|1=Jingū Guards|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11589497}} were employed as guards.
== Ryukyu Shinto Priests == '''Shinin''' (カミンチュ, Kaminchu) is a general term for priests in {{ill|Ryukyuan religion|en|Ryukyuan religion|qid=Q892785|lt=Ryukyu Shinto|1=Ryukyuan religion}}. They also act as medium priests, though {{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|qid=Q11345595|lt=Yuta|1=Yuta (shaman)}}, private diviners, are not considered Shinin.
The phrase "Men are Uminchu (fishermen), Women are Kaminchu (priestesses)" is a traditional saying from {{ill|Kudaka Island|en|Kudaka Island|qid=Q4518835|lt=Kudaka Island|1=Kudaka Island}}, a sacred site of the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Kingdom|1=Ryūkyū Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q60995832}}, reflecting a society where men labored while women served as priests. Until 1979, Kudaka Island performed the priesthood initiation ritual called {{ill|Izaihō|ja|イザイホー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izaihō|1=Izaihō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11266554}}, but the ceremony ceased after 2007 due to a lack of successors.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kyōgoin|ja|供御人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōgoin|1=kugonin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11385494}} * {{ill|Noro (priestess)|en|Noro (priestess)|qid=Q11325791|lt=Noro (priestess)|1=Noro (priestess)}} * {{ill|Yuta (shaman)|en|Yuta (shaman)|qid=Q11345595|lt=Yuta (shaman)|1=Yuta (shaman)}} * {{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|qid=Q1475695|lt=Warrior monks|1=Sōhei}} * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and shrine power|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and shrine power|qq=}} / {{ill|Right of asylum|en|Right of asylum|qid=Q11283415|lt=Right of asylum|1=Right of asylum}} * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Precinct Cities|qq=}}
{{shinto-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|神人|version=101741698|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:神人]]
